skill.name: face-swap
skill.description: Swap a face / character into video or images on RunComfy via the `runcomfy` CLI. Routes across community Wan 2-2 Animate (audio-driven character animation + identity swap), GPT Image 2 Edit (single-shot precise face swap on still images via reference composition), Nano Banana Edit (batch identity-preserving swap), Flux Kontext (single-ref high-fidelity local face edit), and Kling 2-6 Motion Control Pro (transfer motion from one performance onto a target character). Picks the right model for the user's actual intent — single still vs video, full character vs face only, dialog scene vs silent motion. Triggers on "face swap", "swap face", "deepfake", "face replacement", "character swap", "head swap", "put X's face on Y", "make this video star X", "replace the actor in this video", "swap the character in the photo", "deepfake video", "ReActor alternative", or any explicit ask to substitute one identity for another.
skill.summary: Swap a face or character into videos or still images using RunComfy AI models via CLI
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Skill is legitimate AI-powered face swapping documentation for a third-party API service (RunComfy). Static findings are false positives: external commands are hardcoded CLI examples in markdown documentation, not executable code; network access is to documented RunComfy API endpoints only; filesystem access is standard CLI token storage documented transparently. The skill includes appropriate consent warnings for dual-use technology. Tool usage is restricted to `Bash(runcomfy *)` only with shell-injection mitigation via JSON input. No malicious patterns or exfiltration detected.

medium_findings.title: Dual-Use Technology Without Gating
medium_findings.description: Face-swapping is inherently sensitive (deepfake potential) but the skill does not gate inputs - the responsibility rests with the operator. The skill appropriately warns to refuse requests targeting real people without consent, but enforcement depends on operator compliance.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The consent disclosure exists and provides guidance, but the skill cannot enforce compliance. This is an informational concern about the nature of the technology, not a code vulnerability.

low_findings.title: Static Findings False Positive - External Commands
low_findings.description: 76 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' detected in SKILL.md are false positives. All are documentation code blocks showing CLI usage examples (`runcomfy run <model> --input '{...}'`). These are hardcoded, legitimate commands in markdown, not executable code with user-controlled input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every detected instance is within markdown code blocks (```bash) showing documented CLI invocations. No user input is passed to these commands - they are static examples in documentation.

low_findings.title: Static Findings False Positive - Network URLs
low_findings.description: 39 'Hardcoded URL' detections are false positives. All are legitimate RunComfy documentation links (runcomfy.com, docs.runcomfy.com) embedded in markdown to reference supported API endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs point to runcomfy.com domains - the legitimate service the skill integrates with. No malicious exfiltration endpoints detected.

low_findings.title: Static Findings False Positive - Weak Crypto
low_findings.description: 6 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' detections are false positives. The scanner misidentified documentation text about model selection criteria as cryptographic references. No actual cryptographic algorithms or weak crypto usage exists in this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed all flagged lines - none contain cryptographic algorithms. The detections are likely triggered by documentation terms being misidentified.

content.user_title: Swap faces and characters in videos and images with AI
content.value_statement: Face-swapping traditionally requires complex manual editing or expensive specialized software. This skill routes across multiple AI models (Wan 2-2 Animate, GPT Image 2 Edit, Flux Kontext, Kling, Nano Banana) to automatically select the right approach for your use case - video vs still, single shot vs batch, motion-preserving vs identity-preserving.
actual_capabilities: Swap faces from reference images into videos with audio-driven animation
Replace characters in still images using multiple AI models
Transfer motion from one performance video onto a target character
Batch process multiple images with consistent identity preservation
Edit single images with precise face-only changes using text descriptions
Route automatically to the best model based on media type and intent
limitations: Requires RunComfy API access and authentication
Cannot swap faces without a reference image (except Flux Kontext which uses text descriptions)
Batch processing limited to 20 images per call
Video output quality depends on source media quality and reference image clarity

use_cases.title: Create consistent branded content with character identity
use_cases.description: Apply the same spokesperson identity across a product gallery or campaign assets. Use Nano Banana Edit to swap the same reference face into multiple scenes with consistent aspect ratio and resolution.
use_cases.target_user: Marketing teams creating consistent brand content

use_cases.title: Dub video content with new character voices
use_cases.description: Take an existing video with an audio track and replace the character with a new identity that speaks the original audio. Wan 2-2 Animate animates the new character to match the audio track's rhythm and words.
use_cases.target_user: Content creators localizing or repurposing video content

use_cases.title: Produce hero campaign images with precise face placement
use_cases.description: For a single high-impact image, use GPT Image 2 Edit with up to 10 reference images to precisely swap a face from a portrait into a scene while preserving pose, lighting, and background exactly.
use_cases.target_user: Creative directors producing high-value campaign assets

prompt_templates.title: Simple video face swap
prompt_templates.prompt: Swap the face in this video with the face from this image
prompt_templates.scenario: When user provides one video and one reference image for a straightforward face swap

prompt_templates.title: Animated character from audio
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a video where [character name] is speaking with this audio track. Use [image URL] as the character reference.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user wants to animate a character to speak with provided audio

prompt_templates.title: Batch product image identity swap
prompt_templates.prompt: Swap the face in each of these product images with the face from the first image. Keep all other elements unchanged.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user provides multiple images needing the same identity applied consistently

prompt_templates.title: Describe face change without reference
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep everything in this image exactly the same except change only the face to a [description: age, gender, features]
prompt_templates.scenario: When user describes desired face attributes instead of providing a reference image

output_examples.input: Swap the face from portrait.jpg into the video interview.mp4
output_examples.output: The character from portrait.jpg animated to speak with the audio from interview.mp4, with facial movements synchronized to the speech

output_examples.input: Replace the person in scene.jpg with the face from actor.png
output_examples.output: The scene with the actor's face seamlessly integrated, preserving the original pose, lighting, and background

output_examples.input: Change only the face to a 40-year-old man with gray hair and a beard
output_examples.output: The original image with everything preserved except the face replaced according to the description

best_practices: Use high-quality, well-lit reference images with front-facing poses for best face swap results
Match lighting conditions between reference image and target scene to avoid floating faces
Test with simpler single-image swaps first before attempting complex video animations
anti_patterns: Do not attempt to swap faces from low-resolution or heavily compressed images
Avoid swapping faces onto scenes with dramatically different lighting or angles
Do not use this for creating synthetic media of real people without explicit consent

faq.question: What is the difference between Wan 2-2 Animate and Kling Motion Control?
faq.answer: Wan 2-2 Animate uses audio to drive character animation with a reference identity image. Kling Motion Control transfers motion from an existing video performance onto a target character image.

faq.question: Can I swap a face without providing a reference image?
faq.answer: Yes - Flux Kontext Pro allows you to describe the desired face in text instead of providing a reference image. However, for best results, a reference image is recommended.

faq.question: How many images can I process in a batch?
faq.answer: Nano Banana Edit supports 1-20 images per call. The first image is conventionally the identity reference, and remaining images are scenes to swap into.

faq.question: What file formats are supported for input?
faq.answer: Input media must be accessible via HTTPS URLs. Supported types include common image formats (JPG, PNG) and video formats supported by RunComfy models.

faq.question: How do I ensure consistent identity across multiple frames?
faq.answer: Use Nano Banana Edit with the identity as the first image in the image_urls array. Lock aspect_ratio and resolution for batch consistency.

faq.question: What should I do if the swapped face looks unnatural?
faq.answer: Try matching lighting explicitly in your prompt (e.g., 'match skin tone and lighting to image 1') and ensure your reference image has similar quality and pose to the target scene.
